Plants 🌿

Unit 2 on New Curriculum

Learning Competencies 🎯

  • Explain the basic features that make a plant a plant.
  • Tell the difference between plants that have flowers and those that don't.
  • Draw and identify the parts of a plant and its flower.
  • Understand how plants make new plants (reproduce).
  • See how seeds travel and start to grow.
  • Uncover the secrets of photosynthesis, the amazing way plants make their own food.
  • Learn how plants drink water and move food around.
  • Discover how plants respond to their environment (like light and gravity).
  • Investigate some amazing medicinal plants used in Ethiopia.
  • Appreciate the incredible work of Ethiopian botanists.

Characteristics of Plants

What makes a plant a plant? Let's dive into the fantastic features that all plants share. Some of these are common with other living things, but some are totally unique to the plant kingdom! 🌳

  • Living Things: Just like you, plants grow, reproduce, and react to what's happening around them. They are alive!
  • Multicellular: Plants aren't just one single cell. They are made of millions of tiny building blocks called eukaryotic cells. These cells are fancy, with a proper nucleus and other membrane-bound parts.
  • Tough Cell Walls: Imagine having a super strong wall around every cell in your body! Plant cells have this. It's called a cell wall, and it's made of a tough material called cellulose. This gives plants their structure and support.
  • Autotrophic (Self-Feeders): This is a plant's superpower! 🦸‍♀️ They are autotrophs, meaning they make their own food. They use a green pigment called chlorophyll to capture sunlight and cook up a sugary meal. That's why they're also called Producers.
  • Sessile (Fixed in Place): Most plants can't pack up and move. They are sessile, anchored to the ground by their roots. But they're not frozen! Their leaves can turn towards the sun, and their roots can grow towards water.
  • Reproduction: Plants have two main ways of making more plants: asexual reproduction (making a clone of themselves, often with spores) and sexual reproduction (involving the joining of special sex cells called gametes).

Flowering and Non-Flowering Plants

Not all plants show off with pretty flowers! We can split the plant kingdom into two major groups based on whether they produce flowers or not.

Non-Flowering Plants 🌲

These are the old-school plants. They don't have flowers or fruits. They include:

  • Bryophytes (Mosses and Liverworts): These are tiny plants that don't have a proper plumbing system (vascular tissue) to transport water and nutrients. They have to live in damp, shady spots and reproduce using tiny spores.
  • Pteridophytes (Ferns): Ferns are a bit more advanced. They have a vascular system, but they still don't make seeds. Like mosses, they reproduce with spores.
  • Gymnosperms (like Pine Trees): These are "naked seed" plants. They have seeds, but they aren't enclosed in a fruit. Instead, their seeds develop on cones. Think of a pinecone!

Flowering Plants (Angiosperms) 🌸

These are the plants you're probably most familiar with, from roses to apple trees. They are called Angiosperms, which means "covered seed." Their superpower is producing flowers, which then develop into fruits that protect the seeds inside.

Example 1: A Tale of Two Plants

A moss growing on a wet rock is a non-flowering, non-vascular plant. It's small and simple. A giant mango tree is a flowering, vascular plant. It has complex structures like roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits to help it survive and reproduce.

Feature Gymnosperms (Non-Flowering) Angiosperms (Flowering)
Reproductive Structure Cones (like a pinecone) 🌲 Flowers 🌺
Seeds Naked seeds, not inside a fruit. Seeds are protected inside a fruit (like an apple).
Examples Pine trees, Juniper ("Tid"), Podocarpus ("Zigba") Roses, Sunflowers, Avocado, Papaya

Structure and Function of Plant Parts

A typical flowering plant has two main systems working together, like a team, to keep it alive and healthy.

The Shoot System and Root System

The shoot system is everything you see above the ground: the stem, branches, leaves, buds, flowers, and fruits. It's the part that reaches for the sun! ☀️

The root system is the hidden hero underground. It grows downward, anchoring the plant and absorbing water and nutrients from the soil. Unlike stems, roots don't have leaves or buds.

The Leaf: The Plant's Food Factory 🍃

Leaves are designed to be the ultimate solar-powered kitchens. A typical leaf has several parts:

  • Lamina (Blade): The broad, flat part of the leaf that captures sunlight.
  • Petiole: The stalk that connects the leaf to the stem.
  • Midrib and Veins: The leaf's plumbing system, transporting water in and sugar out.

Inside the leaf, there are two main layers:

  • Outer Layer (Epidermis): A single layer of cells acting as the leaf's skin. The top is covered with a waxy cuticle to prevent water loss. The bottom has tiny pores called stomata that open and close to let gases (like CO₂ and O₂) in and out.
  • Middle Layer (Mesophyll): This is where the magic of photosynthesis happens! It has two parts: the palisade layer (packed with chloroplasts to catch sunlight) and the spongy layer (with air spaces to help gases move around).

The Stem: The Plant's Superhighway 🚦

The stem supports the leaves and flowers and acts as a superhighway for transporting materials. A cross-section of a dicot stem reveals several layers:

  • Epidermis: The outer protective layer.
  • Cortex: Stores food.
  • Vascular Bundles: These are like bundles of straws and pipes, arranged in a ring. Each bundle contains xylem (transports water up) and phloem (transports food down).
  • Pith: The central core of the stem, used for storage.

The Root: The Anchor and Lifeline ⚓

Roots anchor the plant and absorb water. There are two main types:

  • Taproots (in Dicots): One large, main root that grows deep into the soil, with smaller branches. Think of a carrot! 🥕
  • Fibrous Roots (in Monocots): A dense network of many thin roots that spread out near the surface. Great for preventing soil erosion! Think of grass roots.

Inside a dicot root, we find layers like the piliferous layer (with root hairs for absorption), a large cortex for storage, and a central core with xylem and phloem for transport.

Difference Dicot Stem Monocot Stem
Vascular Bundles Arranged in a neat ring. Scattered throughout the stem.
Pith Present and well-developed in the center. Absent, no central pith.

Reproduction in Plants

How do plants make new plants? Let's explore their fascinating life cycles.

Reproduction in Non-Flowering Plants (Gymnosperms)

For a pine tree, it all starts with cones. The tree has both small male cones and larger female cones.
1. Pollination: Wind blows pollen from the male cones to the female cones.
2. Fertilization: The pollen grain grows a tube to reach the ovule inside the female cone. A sperm cell travels down the tube and fertilizes the egg, forming a zygote.
3. Seed Development: The zygote develops into an embryo, which is packed inside a seed on the cone's scale.
4. Dispersal & Germination: The cone opens, releasing the seed. If the seed lands in a good spot, it will germinate and grow into a new pine tree.

Reproduction in Flowering Plants (Angiosperms)

Flowers are the reproductive stars of the show for angiosperms! A typical flower has four main parts:

  • Sepals: Green, leaf-like parts that protect the flower bud.
  • Petals: The colorful parts that attract pollinators like bees and butterflies. 🐝
  • Stamen: The male part, which produces pollen.
  • Pistil (or Carpel): The female part, which contains the ovary and ovules.

The life cycle involves several amazing steps:

  1. Pollination: Pollen is transferred from the stamen to the pistil.
  2. Fertilization: A pollen tube grows down to the ovule. Angiosperms have a cool trick called double fertilization, where one sperm fertilizes the egg to make the embryo, and another helps form the endosperm (food for the embryo).
  3. Seed and Fruit Formation: The fertilized ovule becomes a seed, and the ovary around it develops into a fruit. The fruit protects the seed and helps it travel!
  4. Seed Dispersal: Animals, wind, or water carry the fruit and its seeds to new places.
  5. Germination: When conditions are right, the seed sprouts and grows into a new plant.

Seeds: A Plant's Babies

A seed is a baby plant (embryo) packed with a lunchbox (cotyledon or endosperm) and wearing a protective coat (seed coat). Flowering plants have two types of seeds.

Feature Dicot Seed (e.g., Bean) Monocot Seed (e.g., Corn)
Cotyledons Two fleshy cotyledons that store food. One thin cotyledon.
Endosperm Usually absent in the mature seed (food is in cotyledons). Large and well-developed, stores the food.
Root The primary root grows from the radicle. The primary root is replaced by adventitious fibrous roots.

Seed Dispersal and Germination

Plants can't walk, so they need help getting their seeds to new homes. This is seed dispersal, and it prevents overcrowding. Seeds can travel by being eaten by animals and pooped out, by floating on water, or by having wings or hairs to catch the wind. 💨

Germination is when a dormant seed "wakes up" and starts to grow. For this to happen, it needs three things:

  • Water (Moisture): Softens the seed coat and gets the internal chemistry going.
  • Oxygen (Aeration): The seed needs to breathe to get energy for growth.
  • Temperature (Warmth): Seeds have a "just right" temperature for growing.

There are two styles of germination:

  • Epigeal Germination: The cotyledons are pushed above the ground and turn green, acting like the first leaves (e.g., bean plant).
  • Hypogeal Germination: The cotyledons remain below the ground, sending food up to the growing shoot (e.g., corn).

Photosynthesis: The Ultimate Food-Making Process

Photosynthesis is how plants use sunlight to convert carbon dioxide and water into food (glucose, a sugar) and oxygen. It's the most important chemical reaction on Earth! It happens inside the chloroplasts.

The Photosynthesis Equation

Here's the recipe for photosynthesis:

$$ 6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow[Sunlight]{Chlorophyll} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 $$

This means: 6 molecules of Carbon Dioxide + 6 molecules of Water -- (with the help of Chlorophyll and Sunlight) --> 1 molecule of Glucose (sugar) + 6 molecules of Oxygen.

Photosynthesis happens in two stages:

  1. Light-Dependent Reaction: Happens in the grana. Sunlight energy splits water molecules (a process called photolysis), releasing oxygen and creating energy-storing molecules (ATP).
  2. Light-Independent Reaction (Dark Reaction): Happens in the stroma. It uses the energy from the light reaction to combine hydrogen with carbon dioxide to make glucose (sugar).

Transport in Plants

Plants have a sophisticated transport system to move water and food around. This system is made of vascular tissues.

  • Xylem: This tissue is like a bundle of drinking straws. It's made of dead cells that form continuous tubes to transport water and minerals from the roots all the way up to the leaves.
  • Phloem: This tissue is a living pipeline. It transports the sugary food made in the leaves (the source) to all other parts of the plant that need energy, like the roots or fruits (the sinks). This process is called translocation.

The upward pull of water in the xylem is driven by transpiration, which is the evaporation of water from the leaves. As water evaporates from the stomata, it creates a suction force that pulls more water up from the roots, just like sipping through a straw!

Response in Plants (Tropism)

Plants can't run away, but they can move and grow in response to their environment. This response to a directional stimulus is called tropism. It's controlled by a plant hormone called auxin.

  • Phototropism ☀️: Growth in response to light. Shoots are positively phototropic (they grow towards light), while roots are negatively phototropic (they grow away from light).
  • Geotropism (Gravitropism) 🌍: Growth in response to gravity. Roots are positively geotropic (they grow down with gravity), while shoots are negatively geotropic (they grow up against gravity).
  • Hydrotropism 💧: Growth in response to water. Roots are positively hydrotropic (they grow towards water), because they need it to survive!

Medicinal Plants of Ethiopia

Plants are not just food; they are also powerful medicine! For thousands of years, people have used plants to treat illnesses. Ethiopia has a rich tradition of using medicinal plants. More than 60% of the world's population relies on traditional plant-based medicine.

Scientific Name Local Name (Amharic) Part Used Common Treatment
Ruta chalepensis Teenadem Leaf Abdominal pain
Zingiber officinale Jinjibil (Ginger) Rhizome (underground stem) Tonsillitis, cough, abdominal pain
Hagenia abyssinica Ye-kosso Zaf Female flower (Seed) Tapeworm
Nigella sativa Tikur Azmud Seed Intestinal parasites
Ocimum lamifolium Damakesse Leaf Headache, general body illness
Alium sativum Nechshinkurt (Garlic) Bulb Abdominal pain, toothache, common cold
Eucalyptus globulus Nech-Bahir-zaf Leaf Common cold, fever with headache

Renowned Ethiopian Botanist

Ethiopia is home to brilliant scientists who study plants. A leading figure is Professor Sebsebe Demissew. He is a Professor of Plant Systematics and Biodiversity at Addis Ababa University. His lifelong work in studying and conserving Ethiopia's incredible variety of plants has earned him international awards, including the Kew International Medal and the Cuatrecases Medal. In 2018, he became the first Sub-Saharan African scientist to be elected as a Foreign Member of the Royal Society in London in its entire 357-year history! His work helps protect the amazing natural heritage of Ethiopia for future generations. 🌍

Key Concepts to Remember

  • Plants are multicellular, autotrophic (make their own food), and sessile (fixed in place).
  • They are divided into non-flowering (gymnosperms, ferns, mosses) and flowering (angiosperms) groups.
  • Angiosperms protect their seeds inside a fruit, while gymnosperms have "naked" seeds on cones.
  • The shoot system is above ground, and the root system is below ground.
  • Photosynthesis is the process where plants use sunlight, water, and CO₂ to make glucose (food) and oxygen. Its chemical formula is \( 6CO_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \).
  • Xylem transports water up the plant, while phloem transports food throughout the plant.
  • Tropism is a plant's growth response to a stimulus like light (phototropism), gravity (geotropism), or water (hydrotropism).
  • Many plants, like Teenadem and Damakesse in Ethiopia, have important medicinal properties.
